abstract.text: Use exit tests to test functionality that might cause a test process to exit.

inlineContent.text: Your code might contain calls to
or other functions that can cause the current process to exit. For example:

code: func eat(_ food: consuming some Food) {

inlineContent.text: In this function, if
or
is
, the
precondition fails and Swift forces the process to exit. You can write an exit
test to validate preconditions like the ones above and to make sure that your
functions correctly catch invalid inputs.
Exit tests are available on macOS, Linux, FreeBSD, OpenBSD, and Windows.
To create an exit test, call either the
or the
macro:

code: @Test func `Customer won't eat food unless it's delicious`() async {

inlineContent.text: The closure or function reference you pass to the macro is the
body
of the
exit test. When an exit test is performed at runtime, the testing library starts
a new process with the same executable as the current process. The current task
is then suspended (as with
) and waits for the child process to exit.
An exit test cannot run within another exit test.
The parent process doesn’t call the body of the exit test. Instead, the child
process treats the body of the exit test as its
function and calls it
directly.
If the body returns before the child process exits, the process exits as if
returned normally. If the body throws an error, Swift handles it as if
it were thrown from
and forces the process to exit abnormally.
When you create an exit test, specify how you expect the child process exits by
passing an instance of
If you expect the exit test’s body to run to completion or exit normally (for
example, by calling
from the C standard library), pass
If you expect the body to cause the child process to exit abnormally, but the
exact status reported by the system is not important, pass
If you need to check for a specific exit code or signal, pass
or
When the child process exits, the parent process resumes and compares the exit
status of the child process against the expected exit condition you passed. If
they match, the exit test passes; otherwise, it fails and the testing library
records an issue.

content.text: Capture state from the parent process

inlineContent.text: To pass information from the parent process to the child process, you specify
the Swift values you want to pass in a
on the exit test’s body:

code: func `Customer won't eat food unless it's nutritious`(_ food: Food) async {

inlineContent.text: If you use
or
with a
Swift compiler version lower than 6.3, the macro doesn’t capture state.
If a captured value is an argument to the current function or is
, the
compiler infers the value’s type. Otherwise, explicitly specify the type of the
value using the
operator:

code: @Test func `Customer won't eat food unless it's nutritious`() async {
await #expect(processExitsWith: .failure) { [self, food = food as Food] in

inlineContent.text: Every value you capture in an exit test must conform to
and
. The
testing library passes each value to the exit test body by performing the
following steps:
It encodes each value using
in the parent process
It passes the encoded value to the child process
It decodes each value using
in the child process
If a captured value’s type does not conform to both
and
, or
if the value is not explicitly specified in the exit test body’s capture list,
the compiler emits an error:

code: @Test func `Customer won't eat food unless it's nutritious`() async {

content.text: Gather output from the child process

inlineContent.text: The
and
macros
return an instance of
that contains information about the
state of the child process.
By default, the child process is configured without a standard output or
standard error stream. If your test needs to review the content of either of
these streams, pass the key path to the corresponding
property to the macro:

code: func eat(_ food: consuming some Food) {
print("Let's see if I want to eat \(food)...")
@Test func `Customer won't eat food unless it's delicious`() async {

inlineContent.text: The content of the standard output and standard error streams can
contain any arbitrary sequence of bytes, including sequences that aren’t valid
UTF-8 and can’t be decoded by
These streams are globally accessible within the child process, and any code
running in an exit test may write to it including the operating system and any
third-party dependencies you declare in your package description or Xcode
project.
The testing library always sets
to the actual
exit status of the child process (as reported by the system) even if you do not
observe

abstract.text: Create and run tests for your Swift packages and Xcode projects.
An enumeration describing possible status a process will report on exit.
A type describing an exit test.
The possible conditions under which an exit test will complete.
Creates a condition that matches when a process terminates with a given
A condition that matches when a process exits abnormally
Creates a condition that matches when a process exits with a given signal.
A condition that matches when a process exits normally.
A type representing the result of an exit test after it has exited and
returned control to the calling test function.
The exit status reported by the process hosting the exit test.
Check for expected values, outcomes, and asynchronous events in tests.
Check that an expression causes the process to terminate in a given fashion.
Check that an expression causes the process to terminate in a given fashion
and throw an error if it did not.
